learning_outcomes.description: Analyse design concepts and their implications by examining underlying design principles and evaluating their effectiveness in practical applications.
Apply critical thinking to reflect on and analyse the design’s impact and outcomes, evaluating how well they align with user needs.
Create an argument to defend design decisions by integrating theoretical concepts, user feedback, and qualitative and quantitative data to support a compelling design rationale.
Apply foundational knowledge in design principles, theories, and methods within a studio environment and engage in iterative development processes to enhance design outcomes.
Understand ethical considerations in design decisions and apply critical analysis to assess how design choices impact user experiences and cultural contexts.
Apply collaborative tools and techniques to work effectively with team members, clearly communicate design decisions, and document progress to convey design ideas and track development.

assessments.task_description:
This report asks you to present an evidence-based argument that communicates an understanding of the problem space that you are designing for. Working in teams, you will receive and analyse two datasets, developing a clear and coherent argument relating to a potential design direction. You will identify and defend key design insights, propose potential design directions, outline user concerns related to the problem space, and evaluate the likely impact of specific design choices.

The written report will be submitted to Blackboard as a team assessment in PDF or DOCX format.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

Weekly Activities are to be submitted on Blackboard before your scheduled practical. Weekly Activities will be discussed and reviewed during that same class.

Interviews will happen in Week 13 in the practical. Students will need to bring their Weekly Activities to the interview.
assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Oral, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia, Written
assessments.task_description:
Over the course of the semester, you will be working in teams to develop an interactive technology prototype based on an assigned pair of datasets. You will be asked to complete weekly design activities, conduct user research, interrogate design decisions and their potential implications, and document your design reasoning and individual contributions to the team project in a clear, structured manner. These design decisions will be discussed and explored throughout your weekly practical classes. 

This is a sequenced assessment, meaning that you will be asked to submit your work weekly. This will occur before the start of each weekly practical class. 

These tasks will revolve around specific question sets, scenarios, or design challenges. These will be released weekly on Blackboard.

This sequence culminates in an identity verified in-person interview, held during your practical class in week 13. During this interview, you will draw upon your weekly submissions as stimulus material to discuss, justify, and defend your individual design decisions and contributions over the course of the semester.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.category: Creative Production/ Exhibition, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Presentation, Product/ Design
assessments.task_description:
Over the course of this semester you will be working with a team to create design artifacts as part of your exploration of a design space. The final outcome of this iterative design process will be a dynamic interactive website, based on your initial problem framing. The outcome of this assessment will showcase the culmination of your collaborative teamwork and the design justifications made throughout the semester.

During the tradeshow you will be asked to demonstrate how your team has collaborated over the course of multiple iterative design cycles, with the final design artifact serving as evidence of your process. Your submission will include the code-base for your website, records of contributions made by individual team members and supporting documentation to be submitted before the exhibit. This supporting documentation will serve as evidence for your team's contributions. Your demonstration will be graded by two examiners and a post-marking moderation process will be applied.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools
assessments.submission_guidelines: The demonstration will take place during the exhibit, which will be scheduled during studio in Week 13. The final Design Artifact (code and supporting documentation) will be submitted to Blackboard before the studio starts and will be used as part of your team demonstration.

assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.

assessments.task_description:
An important skill-set for designers is to be able to think about the implications of their work. This purpose of this assessment is to offer students the opportunity to think critically about how specific design decisions, features, and interactions impact end users - ethically, culturally, socially, etc.

In this report, you will apply data collected throughout the semester along with both academic and non-academic research to outline and define the potential impact of your design decisions, what negative impacts or harms you're willing to accept, and why. This is intended to be both personal and reflective and will draw upon your weekly activities as a source of evidence.

This report will be submitted via Blackboard in a PDF or DOCX format.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic:
Studios

Studios introduce both theoretical and practical design content, which students explore and apply first through team projects. Support is provided for project work, including assessments, project-based presentations, and exercises. This culminates in the final in-studio tradeshow presentations.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Studios Studios introduce both theoretical and practical design content, which students explore and apply first through team projects. Support is provided for project work, including assessments, project-based presentations, and exercises. This culminates in the final in-studio tradeshow presentations.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic:
Applied Classes

Applied Classes offer activities and tasks that help guide students in developing their skill-set as a designer. These sessions are intended to provide scaffolding to the design process and to get students to reflect on their design choices.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
